risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the randomization process has been described in detail [23]. ... After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group. The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: Although this paper does not restate the exact random method, it explicitly says the randomization process was described in detail elsewhere and the participants were randomized, which is sufficient context to support a proper random allocation sequence rather than a non-random method.

Quote: "The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: This is the only explicit statement about the allocation process. Although the paper does not name a central system or sealed envelopes, blinding of the person who performed group allocation indicates the allocator/recruiter could not foresee the upcoming assignment before enrolment, so allocation concealment is adequately protected.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants were randomized into an exercise or a control group... There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group." Comment: The study does not report that participants or personnel were blinded, and the control-group dropouts preferring the exercise group suggest allocation was likely known; because the intervention was an exercise programme, knowledge of group assignment could plausibly influence self-reported pain/well-being outcomes and engagement in the intervention.

Quote: "The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by the same physiotherapist and the paper explicitly states she/he was blinded to previous measurements. The key outcomes here include objective/standardized measures (goniometer ROM, dynamometer strength, step-test heart rate/power) and patient questionnaire outcomes (CHAQ/CHQ, pain VAS). Because the outcome assessment for the measured tests was blinded and the main subjective questionnaires were self-reported by participants, the paper provides sufficient evidence that knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement for this study overall.

Quote: "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization... There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time." Comment: The paper reports 54 randomized children, but follow-up numbers are substantially lower and imbalanced across arms at outcome assessment (e.g., Table 4 shows Exercise group n=26 vs Control group n=19 for repeated measures; Table 7 shows CHAQ n=20 vs 15 and CHQ n=23 vs 16). Most importantly, the text explicitly states 10 control-group dropouts after randomization, creating differential attrition well above the 10 percentage-point threshold and making attrition bias likely. Reasons given (preference for exercise group; lack of time) do not remove the imbalance, and no convincing missing-data method (e.g., multiple imputation/sensitivity analysis) is reported.
